1: κατάκριμα (katakrima) is the adverse verdict — the condemning sentence, not merely the feeling of guilt. Its negation here is forensic: the courtroom record has been cleared. The construction οὐδὲν κατάκριμα τοῖς ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ sets "in Christ Jesus" as the jurisdictional sphere where the verdict does not hold; the prepositional phrase does structural work the English "for" can obscure.
2: νόμος appears twice with different referents: the "law of the Spirit of life" and the "law of sin and death." The word operates here as "governing principle" rather than Torah; the genitive "of life" marks the Spirit as the source of that life. The perfect ἠλευθέρωσεν (has set free) describes an accomplished liberation whose effects continue to hold.
3:
The phrase περὶ ἁμαρτίας (peri hamartias) is the LXX technical term for the sin offering (Lev 5:7; 7:2). Paul is placing the Incarnation inside the sacrificial framework of Torah. The phrase ἐν ὁμοιώματι σαρκὸς ἁμαρτίας (in the likeness of sinful flesh) is careful: ὁμοίωμα (likeness) means Christ shares the form of fallen humanity without being constituted by its sin — real solidarity, hedged identity.

κατέκρινεν τὴν ἁμαρτίαν ἐν τῇ σαρκί — he condemned sin in the flesh: the condemnation happens inside the very sphere sin had occupied.
4: δικαίωμα (dikaiōma) — a specific righteous requirement or ordinance, not the general quality of righteousness (dikaiosynē). The law's dikaiōma is now fulfilled ἐν ἡμῖν (in us) — passive, not active: it is accomplished in us, not by us. The present participle περιπατοῦσιν (who walk) identifies ongoing orientation rather than perfect performance.
5: φρόνημα (phronēma) — mind-set, inclination, or habitual orientation, not simply "mind" or "thoughts." The verb φρονέω covers the whole set of one's mental dispositions and longings. Those who are κατὰ σάρκα (according to the flesh) are oriented toward what the flesh prizes; the contrast with κατὰ πνεῦμα is totalizing — about which domain governs the person's entire orientation.
6: φρόνημα σαρκός is death; φρόνημα πνεύματος is life and peace. The Greek places the predicate nominatives without a linking verb in the second clause (asyndeton), making the identification stark: this orientation is death; that orientation is life and peace. The double outcome "life and peace" is covenantal shorthand for eschatological blessing (cf. Num 25:12; Isa 54:10).
7: ἔχθρα εἰς θεόν — enmity toward God, not merely estrangement from God. The flesh's mind-set is positionally hostile; it does not merely fail to obey but is constitutionally opposed (οὐχ ὑποτάσσεται, does not submit). The double οὐ δύναται (cannot) marks impossibility, not merely difficulty.
8: οἱ ἐν σαρκί (those in the flesh) does not refer to embodied existence per se — Paul assumes Christians remain embodied — but to those whose identity is located in and governed by the sphere of σάρξ. The inability to please God is structural, not situational.
9: εἴπερ (since indeed) — a first-class conditional that treats the condition as factually assumed. Paul does not say "if the Spirit happens to live in you"; the eiper treats it as established. The equation "Spirit of God" = "Spirit of Christ" is stated as a matter of course, presupposing Trinitarian identification of the Spirit's source and person.
10: Two parallel διά constructions with accusative (causative): the body is dead διὰ ἁμαρτίαν (because of sin), but the spirit is alive διὰ δικαιοσύνην (because of righteousness). The lower-case "spirit" here most naturally refers to the human spirit — the inner person — being animated by God's righteousness, not to the Holy Spirit (contrast v. 9).
11: The argument moves from past (the Spirit raised Christ) to future (the Spirit will also give life to mortal bodies). The prepositional phrase διὰ τοῦ ἐνοικοῦντος αὐτοῦ πνεύματος (through his Spirit who dwells in you) uses the genitive of means: the Spirit is the agent of the bodily resurrection. This is the passage's strongest statement connecting pneumatology to bodily resurrection.
12: ὀφειλέται (debtors/those under obligation) — the debt metaphor; Paul uses it negatively here. Having been freed from the flesh's governance creates no debt to the flesh. The verse prepares the logic of vv. 13–14: obligation runs in the other direction entirely.
13: θανατοῦτε (put to death) — present active imperative, indicating ongoing action: keep mortifying the practices of the body. The agent of this mortification is πνεύματι (by the Spirit) — Spirit-empowered discipline, not autonomous effort. The antithesis is stark: live according to the flesh and you die; mortify the flesh's practices by the Spirit and you live.
14: ἄγονται (are led) — present passive: those who are in an ongoing state of being led by the Spirit of God — these are υἱοὶ θεοῦ (sons of God). The participle ὅσοι (as many as) is inclusive: sonship is defined by Spirit-leadership rather than by ethnic identity.
15: υἱοθεσίας (huiothesias) — adoption/placing-as-son; a legal-relational term found only in Paul in the NT (here, 8:23; 9:4; Gal 4:5; Eph 1:5). The contrast with πνεῦμα δουλείας (spirit of slavery) maps onto the Exodus pattern: slavery gave way to covenant sonship. Ἀββᾶ ὁ πατήρ — the Aramaic address Abba followed by its Greek equivalent; the bilingual cry is not liturgical formula but the spontaneous address of intimacy, the same term Jesus uses in Gethsemane (Mark 14:36).
16: συμμαρτυρεῖ (co-witnesses, bears witness alongside) — the Spirit's testimony is not separate from our spirit's cry but joint: our spirit cries "Abba" and the Holy Spirit witnesses alongside that cry, confirming its validity. The syn- prefix marks participation, not replacement.
17: κληρονόμοι (heirs) + συγκληρονόμοι (co-heirs with Christ) — four syn- compound verbs in vv. 17–29 mark coparticipation: sympaschomen (suffer-together), syndoxasthōmen (glorified-together). The condition εἴπερ συμπάσχομεν uses the same eiper as v. 9 — treating shared suffering as the present form that shared glory will follow.
18: λογίζομαι (I reckon/calculate) — a deliberate act of estimation, not merely an opinion; Paul uses the verb elsewhere for accounting transactions (4:3–8). The present sufferings are subjected to rational evaluation and judged not comparable. ἀποκαλυφθῆναι — future passive infinitive; the glory is hidden, awaiting eschatological uncovering.
19: ἀποκαραδοκία (apokaradokia) — a rare compound; literally, watching with head stretched forward in tense expectation. The creation is not passive but actively straining toward the horizon — one of only two NT occurrences (also Phil 1:20). The object of the longing is τὴν ἀποκάλυψιν τῶν υἱῶν τοῦ θεοῦ (the revelation of the sons of God), linking vv. 14–17 to this cosmic frame.
20: ματαιότης (mataiotes) — futility, emptiness, vanity; the same word used throughout LXX Ecclesiastes for hebel (vapor/vanity) in post-fall existence. The creation was subjected οὐχ ἑκοῦσα (not willingly), marking this condition as alien to its created purpose. The subjection is ἐφ' ἑλπίδι (upon/in hope) — hope is a structural component of the subjection, not an added comfort.
21: δουλεία τῆς φθορᾶς (slavery of corruption/decay) — phthora covers both physical decay and moral corruption; the liberation promised is from both. The telos is ἐλευθερία τῆς δόξης τῶν τέκνων τοῦ θεοῦ — three stacked genitives compounding the richness of the eschatological promise.
22: συστενάζει and συνωδίνει — two syn- compound verbs: the whole creation groans together and is in labor-pain together ἄχρι τοῦ νῦν (until now). The birth-labor metaphor is not merely suffering but productive suffering pointing toward a definite end — the same image Paul uses for the labor pains of the final age in 1 Thess 5:3.
23: ἀπαρχὴν τοῦ πνεύματος — the firstfruits of the Spirit; the Spirit is possessed now as the initial installment of the full harvest to come (cf. arrabōn, down payment, in 2 Cor 1:22). υἱοθεσίαν reappears (cf. v. 15) but now as something still awaited — specifically τὴν ἀπολύτρωσιν τοῦ σώματος (the redemption of the body). Adoption is both now (v. 15) and not yet (v. 23); its bodily dimension remains outstanding.
24: τῇ ἐλπίδι ἐσώθημεν — we were saved in/by hope. The aorist passive marks salvation as an accomplished event, but hope is the modality in which that salvation is currently held. Hope that sees its object is no longer hope — ἐλπιζόμενον (what is hoped for) by definition lies beyond present sight.
25: δι' ὑπομονῆς (through patient endurance) — hypomonē is not passive resignation but active steadfastness under pressure; it recurs in Paul as the characteristic posture of those whose salvation is held eschatologically. The present ἀπεκδεχόμεθα (we are eagerly awaiting) picks up the cosmic waiting of vv. 19–23.
26: ὑπερεντυγχάνει (intercedes for us) — a NT hapax; the hyper- prefix intensifies the intercession: the Spirit intervenes with a force that transcends our own prayer capacity. στεναγμοῖς ἀλαλήτοις (unutterable/wordless groans) — alalētos means either "inexpressible" (groans one cannot articulate) or "unuttered" (the Spirit's groans are beyond human speech); the latter coheres better with the Spirit as the one interceding rather than the one through whom we speak.
27: ὁ ἐρευνῶν τὰς καρδίας (the one who searches the hearts) — a divine attribute from Jer 17:10 and Ps 139; here applied to the Father who knows the mind of the Spirit because the Spirit intercedes κατὰ θεόν (in accordance with God). The Spirit's intercession is therefore perfectly effective — never out of alignment with the divine purpose.
28: πάντα συνεργεῖ — all things work together. A significant variant in manuscript D and some patristic sources reads "God works all things together with those who love him," changing the subject from "all things" to "God." Either reading is grammatically possible; the context (God as active subject in vv. 29–30) slightly favors the variant. κατὰ πρόθεσιν (according to purpose) — prothesis means a prior-setting-forth, a deliberate plan; it anticipates the vocabulary of vv. 29–30.
29: προέγνω (foreknew) — the verb proginōskō in Pauline usage implies relational knowledge, not merely prior cognition. In OT idiom, to "know" someone is to enter covenant relation (cf. Amos 3:2); the pro- adds temporal priority. εἰκόνος τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ (the image of his Son) — the goal of predestination is conformity to Christ, recovering the imago Dei compromised at Adam (Gen 1:26). πρωτότοκον ἐν πολλοῖς ἀδελφοῖς — firstborn among many siblings: Christ is the eldest of a family, not an isolated figure.
30: The golden chain: προορίζω → καλέω → δικαιόω → δοξάζω (predestined → called → justified → glorified). All four verbs are aorist: glorification is stated alongside calling and justification as accomplished from the divine standpoint. The proleptic aorist treats the not-yet-accomplished glorification as so certain it can be narrated in the same past tense as the rest. The chain has no gaps.
31: The rhetorical unit (vv. 31–39) proceeds by a series of unanswerable questions. The opening εἰ ὁ θεὸς ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν (if God is for us) is not a conditional doubt but a conditional foundation — since God is for us, the questions that follow have no threatening answer. The argument moves from particular (God gave the Son) to universal (he will withhold no lesser gift).
32: τοῦ ἰδίου υἱοῦ οὐκ ἐφείσατο (did not spare his own Son) — the verb pheidomai (to spare) echoes LXX Gen 22:16, where God commends Abraham for not sparing his son Isaac. The verbal parallel is deliberate: the Father acts as Abraham acted — and goes further, actually delivering his Son where Abraham was stopped. The argument runs from greater to lesser: if he gave the greatest gift, no lesser gift will be withheld.
33: ἐγκαλέσει (will bring a charge) — the future of enkaleō, a forensic term for lodging a legal accusation. The answer is not "no one" but "God who justifies": the judge himself has already rendered a favorable verdict. A charge against the justified is self-defeating; the one before whom the charge would be brought is the one who declared them righteous.
34: τίς ὁ κατακρινῶν (who is the one who will condemn) — the future participle; the anticipated condemner is answered by four statements about Christ: he died, more than that he was raised, he is at God's right hand, and he intercedes for us. The final clause (ὃς καὶ ἐντυγχάνει ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν) places Christ's heavenly intercession as the present answer to every potential condemnation.
35: τίς ἡμᾶς χωρίσει ἀπὸ τῆς ἀγάπης τοῦ Χριστοῦ — seven pressures are listed: θλῖψις (trouble), στενοχωρία (hardship), διωγμός (persecution), λιμός (famine), γυμνότης (nakedness), κίνδυνος (danger), μάχαιρα (sword). Each was a material reality for Paul's readers, not a hypothetical; the list anchors the argument in the known experience of the Roman communities.
36: The Psalm 44:22 (LXX 43:23) quotation — ἕνεκέν σου θανατούμεθα ὅλην τὴν ἡμέραν (for your sake we are being killed all day long) — identifies the suffering of v. 35 with the covenant community's experience throughout Israel's history. Being counted as sheep for slaughter is not an anomaly but the scriptural pattern of the righteous under pressure; Paul cites it to prevent the community from reading suffering as evidence of divine abandonment.
37: ὑπερνικῶμεν (we more than conquer) — a NT hapax; the hyper- prefix intensifies ordinary conquest. The victory is not despite the sufferings of v. 35 but through them (ἐν τούτοις, in all these things) and not by self-strength but διὰ τοῦ ἀγαπήσαντος ἡμᾶς (through him who loved us).
38: The catalog of potential separators expands beyond personal suffering: θάνατος (death), ζωή (life), ἄγγελοι (angels), ἀρχαί (principalities), ἐνεστῶτα (things present), μέλλοντα (things to come), δυνάμεις (powers). The inclusion of "life" alongside "death" is notable: even the attachments and demands of life cannot sever the bond.
39: ὕψωμα and βάθος (height and depth) — likely astronomical terms for the zenith and nadir of celestial bodies in Paul's cosmological vocabulary. The final οὔτε τις κτίσις ἑτέρα (nor any other created thing) closes the catalog comprehensively: nothing within creation — no cosmic power, no temporal category, no spatial dimension — can accomplish the separation. The love of God is the one uncreated constant.
